title: pMapLimit swallows all per-item errors unconditionally with .catch(() => {})
description: The exported pMapLimit utility catches and discards every error thrown by the callback fn. Callers such as rebuildIndex and removeOrphans rely on this but have no way to detect partial failures: a readFile or parseManifest exception is silently dropped, meaning the function returns as if the item was successfully processed. This can hide data-loss conditions (e.g. a manifest that errors during removeOrphans reference collection) and is different from the explicit parseErrors counter that removeOrphans maintains outside pMapLimit.

title: getCachedSsimFn outer catch swallows the exception type, collapsing import errors with logic errors
description: The outer try/catch around the dynamic import of ssim.js sets cachedSsim = null for any thrown value, including non-import exceptions (e.g. a TypeError from bad property access on ssimMod). Because cachedSsim is then cached as null permanently, a transient import glitch or a programming error both silently degrade all subsequent convergence profiling to ssim-less mode. The inner .catch(() => null) already handles import failure; the outer catch is redundant and masks real errors.

title: measureConvergenceProfile calls dispose() in both catch and finally, risking double-dispose of the decoder
description: In measureConvergenceProfile the decoder's dispose() is called inside the catch block (line ~372) and again unconditionally in the finally block (line ~375). If the WASM decoder's dispose() is not idempotent this will throw on the second call. More concretely, if the catch path disposes and then rethrows (return undefined), the finally block also runs and disposes again. The comparator is also disposed in finally only, which is correct, but the decoder pattern is inconsistent with decodeFinal which correctly disposes only in finally.

category: unchecked return value / missing null check
title: Test calls imageIdForPath synchronously (no await) — always receives a Promise as imageId
description: imageIdForPath is an async function returning Promise<string>. On line 60, the test does const imageId = imageIdForPath(master) without await, so imageId is always a Promise object. The subsequent readFile call uses this Promise as a path component which will silently produce a path like 'out/images/[object Promise]/manifest.json' — not the actual imageId. The test appears to be checking the wrong directory and may never exercise the real written manifest.

category: unchecked return value / missing null check
title: Second test also calls imageIdForPath without await — same Promise-as-path bug
description: Same as errors-4: the 'standard RAW ingest' test also calls imageIdForPath(master) without await on line 85, so imageId is a Promise. Any assertion on manifest.levels or full.tiled is evaluating data from a non-existent path (the readFile will throw or read the wrong file), silently voiding the test coverage.

title: ingestImage return type is IngestResult but test asserts it equals the string 'written'
description: ingestImage returns Promise<IngestResult> (an object with {outcome, stagedBytes?, plan?, degraded?}), not a string. The test does expect(await ingestImage(...)).toBe('written') which compares an IngestResult object to a string — this will always fail if the type is correct. The test should assert (await ingestImage(...)).outcome === 'written'. This indicates the test was written for an older API signature and was not updated when IngestResult was introduced.
code_excerpt: expect(await ingestImage(master, backends, { outDir: out })).toBe("written");

title: acquireWriteLockFile has no timeout on the waitForReadLocksToClear inner loop when a live reader refuses to release
description: The waitForReadLocksToClear deadline is computed as start + timeoutMs. However the outer loop in acquireWriteLockFile can spend considerable time retrying stale-lock detection and sleeping before the wx-create succeeds, leaving very little of the original timeoutMs budget for the reader-drain phase. In pathological cases (e.g. many stale locks, slow FS) the effective read-drain timeout can approach zero, causing an immediate timeout error even though the total elapsed time is within bounds. This is not a hard correctness bug but can produce spurious lock-timeout failures under load.

title: Runlog append error is swallowed by empty outer catch, silently losing the run record
description: The runlog append block (O6) wraps the entire read-parse-push-write sequence in try/catch {} with no logging or metric emission. If the runlog file is corrupt JSON, or a write fails (ENOSPC, permissions), the error is silently discarded. Since the runlog is the audit trail for batch operations, silent loss is a correctness concern especially for recovery via --resume --retry-failed which relies on the checkpoint, not the runlog, but operators inspecting the runlog would see incomplete history.

title: rm subcommand does not release imgLock on error paths (before acquireWriteLock for rebuildIndex)
description: In the rm branch, acquireImageWriteLock is called and stored in imgLock, then removeImage is awaited. If removeImage throws, imgLock.release() is never called because there is no try/finally around it. The heldLock (gallery-level write lock) is released by the outer finally, but the per-image lock is leaked. On crash recovery the stale-detection (24h or dead PID) will eventually clean it, but concurrent operations on the same image will block for up to 30s.

title: buildManifest computes aspect = width / height without guarding height === 0
description: If a caller passes height: 0 (e.g. from a corrupted decode result returning zero dimensions), aspect becomes Infinity. The Zod schema's z.number().finite().positive() will catch this at parse time and throw, but the error message will be a Zod validation error on 'aspect' rather than a clear 'zero height' message, making debugging harder. More importantly the error propagates after the manifest is already constructed, leaving no opportunity to log useful context.
code_excerpt: aspect: round4(args.width / args.height),

title: Per-pass ssim and butteraugli computation errors are silently swallowed inside measureConvergenceProfile
description: Both the ssimFn call (line ~331) and the comparator.compare / computeButteraugli calls (line ~336-343) are wrapped in bare catch {} blocks. If ssim.js or the WASM butteraugli comparator throws due to a programming error or type mismatch, the pass is silently omitted from the curve with no indication. This could produce a qualityCurve with far fewer points than expected and mislead the manifest consumer about convergence, without any warning in telemetry.

title: MAX_MASTER_BYTES size-guard error message exposes the full internal limit constant and actual file size in user-facing output
description: The error thrown for oversized masters includes both the raw byte count of the file and the internal MAX_MASTER_BYTES constant (536870912). While not a security issue for a local CLI tool, it is an information-leakage pattern: a caller using this as a library (or piping --json) sees raw internal numeric limits rather than a human-friendly message. A more informative message would state the size in MB and round the limit.

title: rebuildIndex does not handle ENOENT on the index rename — a concurrent delete of the tmp file causes silent data loss
description: In rebuildIndex, the tmp file is written then renamed. If two concurrent rebuildIndex calls race and one deletes the other's tmp file (e.g. via unlink on EEXIST in writeFileAtomic at some other path), the second rename will get ENOENT and the function throws, leaving no index.json update. The function has no EEXIST guard on the rename (unlike ingest.ts's writeFileAtomic which handles EEXIST). Under the gallery write lock this race is unlikely but possible if the lock is advisory-only and non-cooperative code runs.

title: No structured error taxonomy: all failures use plain Error, making programmatic error handling fragile
description: All error paths across ingest.ts, lock.ts, checkpoint.ts, and migrate.ts throw plain Error objects with string messages. Callers in batch dispatchers distinguish errors only by checking err instanceof Error or string-matching err.message (e.g. 'failed to acquire image write lock', 'ingest timeout', 'master too large'). A small IngestError class hierarchy (or at minimum a code property convention like { code: 'LOCK_TIMEOUT' | 'MASTER_TOO_LARGE' | 'BUDGET_EXCEEDED' }) would let callers classify errors without fragile string matching and improve structured logging in --json mode.

title: Per-image failure events emit message and code but not stack or error type, limiting post-mortem debugging
description: When an image fails in the batch loop, the image-failed telemetry event includes error message and optional code but drops the stack trace. In --json mode operators see only a string message for decode/encode WASM errors which may be opaque (e.g. 'unreachable executed'). Including err.stack in the event payload (gated on a --verbose or --json-debug flag) would allow post-mortem triage of WASM panics without requiring a separate stderr scan.

category: missing null check / unbounded input assumption
title: imageIdForPath is called inside a catch block with an empty catch body — errors during ID derivation for rm are silently ignored
description: In the rm branch, if target looks like a path (contains '/' or '\' or '.'), imageIdForPath is called inside try { imageId = await imageIdForPath(target); } catch {}. If imageIdForPath fails (e.g. the path does not exist on disk causing realpath to fail), the catch swallows the error and imageId remains set to the original target string. This means the rm operation proceeds with a path string as the imageId, creating an images/<path-string>/ directory lookup that silently returns 'nothing to do'.

title: waitForReadLocksToClear polls at 50-100ms fixed interval with no exponential backoff — can generate excessive FS I/O for large reader counts
description: The read-lock-drain loop polls every 50-100ms (sleep(50 + Math.random() * 50)) until all live read locks clear or the deadline is hit. With many concurrent readers on a network FS, this generates O(readers) stat/readFile calls per poll cycle. A simple exponential backoff (capped at e.g. 500ms) would reduce FS load without materially increasing worst-case drain time.

title: measureConvergenceProfile (via backends.ts) uses bytesPushed as curve point bytes but resets it per-chunk — points reflect chunk-offset not absolute byte offset within the full file after the first chunk
description: QUESTION: In measureConvergenceProfile (backends.ts line ~346), each curve point records bytes: bytesPushed. bytesPushed is accumulated across chunks (bytesPushed += chunk.length), so it should correctly represent the cumulative offset at the time each progress event fires. However, progress events fire asynchronously via the drain coroutine, and bytesPushed is updated in the push loop — there is a potential ordering question about whether the progress event fires before or after bytesPushed has advanced to the next chunk. If the decoder emits a progress event for data that was received in chunk N before the JS engine increments bytesPushed for chunk N+1, the curve point will be correct. But if events are deferred to after the next push, the bytes field may record a later offset. Verify the actual event ordering.

title: plan! non-null assertion on line 506 can throw if buildFallbackPlan unexpectedly returns null-like
description: applyIngestPlan(plan!, backends, opts) uses a non-null assertion. plan is set inside conditional branches: nativeFmt path sets it, fallback path sets it, jpg path sets it, else throws. However the else-accept path assigns plan = await buildFallbackPlan(...) which never returns null (it always returns an IngestPlan or throws). The non-null assertion is therefore defensive but the TypeScript type of plan is IngestPlan | null (initialized as null, not undefined), so if a future code path accidentally falls through without assigning plan, the assertion will throw a cryptic TypeError rather than a meaningful error.
code_excerpt:
await applyIngestPlan(plan!, backends, opts);
    const stagedBytes = (plan!.levels || []).reduce((s: number, lv: any) => s + (lv.stagedBytes || 0), 0);

title: createJxlBackend methods have no timeout on WASM calls — a hung WASM encoder will block indefinitely
description: All JxlBackend methods (encodePyramid, encodeTileContainer, downscaleRgba8, transcodeJpeg, etc.) call into WASM without any timeout guard. If the WASM module enters an infinite loop or deadlocks (e.g. due to a libjxl encoder bug at a particular image dimension), the Node.js worker thread will hang forever. The per-image timeoutMs mechanism in ingestImage uses Promise.race with a setTimeout, which does work, but only if the WASM call itself is wrapped in a promise that can be raced — since push() is synchronous and the encoder is called via await enc(...), a truly blocked WASM call would hold the event loop and prevent the timeout from firing. Adding a worker-level watchdog timeout in ingest-worker.ts would provide defense in depth.
